segments.s431-g105: همی is an adverbial iterative marker meaning 'continually/always' placed before the verb. خوار + داشتن is a light-verb construction: خوار is the complement 'despised/low' and داری (2nd-singular present of داشتن) is the verb 'you hold/you have', so together خوار داری تو means 'you hold (something) in contempt' with تو as the explicit subject (postposed for emphasis). گفتار من is the object 'my speech/words' placed after the verb.
segments.s431-g106: به + خیره forms an adverbial phrase 'by/with a fixed stare' (به used here as an instrumental/means marker). بجویی is a 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive form (poetic usage with the prefix ب- instead of modern می-) meaning 'you seek'. تو again is the subject (postposed). آزار من is the object 'my harm/annoyance' (آزار as noun = 'harm'); the clause as a whole means 'you, by staring, seek to cause me harm'.
segments.s431-g107: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed poetic word order: چنین 'thus' + داد (past of دادن, here 'gave') + پاسخ 'answer' = 'he gave answer thus'. که introduces the content clause or indirect speech. چند here is an interrogative 'how long / how much'; از is the preposition 'from/of' linking to فریب 'deceit', so چند از فریب functions as a rhetorical question roughly 'how long (will you continue) from/of deceit' = 'how long will you persist in deceit'.
segments.s431-g108: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/indeed'. به تنگ is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'into a narrow (place)' with به = 'into/towards' and تنگ = 'narrow'. اندر آمد is the past 'came into' (اندر 'in/within' + آمد 'came'); نشیب 'descent/slope' is the noun that has come into that narrowness and is placed after the verb (poetic inversion). The clause means 'truly the descent has come into a narrow pass/space.'
segments.s431-g9: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning "when"; بشنید is a past-tense, 3rd-person-singular verb "he heard". In this poetic word order the direct object آوازش (his voice) comes before the subject اسفندیار, so the clause reads literally "When his voice Esfandiyar heard" = "When Esfandiyar heard his voice."
segments.s431-g10: سلیح جهان is a noun phrase "the weapon/armament of the world"; پیش او is a prepositional phrase "in front of him/in his presence." گشت is the past-tense of شدن "to become" used here as "became," and خوار is a predicative adjective "despised/worthless." The clause means "(the world's armament) became despised before him."
segments.s431-g11: چنین گفت پس با پشوتن is a reporting clause: چنین گفت پس = "thus he then said," با + proper name means "to/with Peshotan" (i.e., he spoke to Peshotan). که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. Note: گفت is the past 3rd-person of گفتن "to say."
segments.s431-g12: بپیچد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive-form (with poetic prefix بــ) expressing the potential/optative sense "may turn/turn away." ز introduces the source/point of departure "from," followed by چنگال مرد دلیر "the claw of the brave man." Word order places the verb before the prepositional phrase: "(that) the lion may turn from the claw of the brave man."
segments.s431-g13: گمانی is a noun "a supposition/guess;" نبردم is a past 1st-person negative form meaning "I did not [take/have]" and here functions idiomatically with گمانی to mean "I did not suppose/think." که introduces the subordinate clause naming what was not supposed: "that Rostam..."
segments.s431-g14: به ایوان is a directional phrase "into/toward the iwan (portico)." کشد is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive (after که) of کشیدن "to pull/drag/bring," here expressing the action Rostam would perform. The list ببر و گبر و کلاه are three direct objects coordinated by و: "a tiger, an infidel, and a hat." The clause reads "that Rostam would drag into the portico a tiger and an infidel and a hat."
segments.s431-g15: همان is a determiner "that same/that very;" بارکش is an epithet "load-bearing/pack-carrying" applied to رخش (Rakhsh, the steed). زیراندرش is a fused adverbial phrase meaning "beneath him/under it." Together they identify the horse and its position relative to the rider.
segments.s431-g16: ز پیکان is an ablative phrase "from arrows/by arrows;" نبود is the past negative "there was not/was absent." ایچ پیدا is "anything visible/any sign" (ایچ = nothing/anything, پیدا = visible/apparent). برش here means "on him" (بر + ـش), so the whole clause says that "there was not any arrow visible on him." This line uses older/poetic contractions (برش) for "on him."
segments.s431-g65: چنین داد پاسخ is a compact past-tense clause meaning “(he) gave such an answer.” داد is the past simple ‘gave,’ placed before the object پاسخ (answer) in poetic word order. که introduces the reported content clause that follows. مرد فریب is a noun‑noun construction in which فریب (deceit) qualifies مرد: literally “a man of deceit,” i.e. “a deceitful man.”
segments.s431-g66: This phrase is a compressed, paradoxical description of that man. The sequence is nominal and elliptical (no finite verb): نیم روز پرخاش و روز نهیب lists two appositive phrases modifying مرد فریب—‘half a day (of) reproach’ and ‘a day (of) rebuke.’ In classical/poetic Persian such noun phrases often stand without an explicit copula and describe habitual character or behaviour.
segments.s431-g67: اگر introduces a conditional: اگر زنده خواهی (“if you wish to be alive” / “if you would live”). خواهی is the present/future-form ‘you want/wish’; که introduces the subordinate clause ماند به جای (‘remain in place’). The order is typical classical Persian: protasis (اگر … خواهی) followed by the content clause begun with که.
segments.s431-g68: نخستین سخن is the direct object ‘the first word.’ بند here is an imperative meaning ‘bind’ or ‘hold back’ (poetic imperative form equivalent to ‘ببند’/‘مبساط’), so ‘hold back your first word.’ بر نه به پای is an idiomatic prohibition: بر نه = ‘do not place/put on/bring forward’ and به پای ‘to the foot/forward’ = ‘do not advance your foot’ (i.e. do not step forward). The whole is a twofold admonition: restrain your first speech and do not advance.
segments.s431-g69: از ایوان و خان is a locative/ablative phrase ‘from the iwan (porch) and the khan (inn).’ چند modifies the verb/act expressed by گویی; in classical Persian گویی functions as ‘you say/you speak’ (here interrogative ‘how often do you speak’). همی is the archaic continuous/intonational particle (marks habitual/ongoing action).
segments.s431-g70: رخ آشتی را is a noun phrase with را marking the definite object: ‘the face of reconciliation.’ بشویی is the present/subjunctive form of شستن (to wash) with the b‑prefix typical in classical Persian; here it means ‘you wash/you cleanse’ (used figuratively). همی again gives the habitual/continuous flavor: ‘you are always washing the face of reconciliation’ (i.e. always feigning or polishing reconciliation).
segments.s431-g71: دگر باره is an adverbial expression ‘again/once more.’ The clause follows subject–object–verb order typical in Persian: رستم (subject) + زبان (object: ‘tongue/speech’) + برگشاد (verb phrase ‘opened’ = past tense ‘opened up’): ‘Rostam opened his tongue’ = ‘Rostam spoke again.’
segments.s431-g72: مکن is the negative imperative ‘do not.’ شهریارا is the vocative form ‘O king.’ ز is the classical/prepositional ‘of/from/about,’ so ز بیداد یاد is an idiomatic object phrase ‘(the) remembrance/mention of injustice.’ The whole imperative addresses the king: ‘Do not, O king, mention/recall injustice.’
segments.s431-g1: This phrase begins with the subject noun سپیده (dawn) followed by the temporal adverb همانگه 'at that moment'. The prepositional cluster ز که بر دمید is a poetic combination meaning 'from/when it breathed/rose' — که here functions as a subordinating particle ('that/when') and دمید is a past (narrative) form meaning 'dawned/rose'. Note the verb-first feel in epic Persian is avoided here; the whole clause sets the time/action of dawn rising.
segments.s431-g2: میان شب تیره اندر is a locative phrase 'into the middle of the dark night' (میان = middle/in the midst, تیره = dark). اندر is the older/pre‑classical preposition 'in/within'. چمید is the verb describing what the dawn did in that place; the exact poetic sense of چمید is obscure here and not covered by the listed noun senses, so it is left as an untranslated verbal action 'shone/appeared'.
segments.s431-g3: بپوشید is a past/preterite-form (classical/epic style) meaning 'he put on' or 'he donned'. The personal name رستم follows the verb (V S O order is common in older Persian narrative), and سلیح نبرد is a noun phrase 'battle-weapon/war-gear' (سلیح = arms, نبرد qualifying it as for battle).
segments.s431-g4: همی is an older adverb giving a continuous or iterative sense ('kept on/continually'). یاد کردن is the light-verb construction 'to remember' where یاد is the noun 'remembrance' and کرد is the past of کردن 'to do', here functioning as the verb 'remembered'. از + جهان‌آفرین marks the object/target of remembrance ('of the world-creator').
segments.s431-g5: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal clause; آمد is the simple past 'came'. بر + لشکر = 'upon the army' (approached/arrived at the host) and نامدار is a postpositive adjective 'renowned/illustrious'. Word order is classical: subordinating particle + verb + prepositional phrase + adjective after its noun.
segments.s431-g6: که here is a relative pronoun 'that/who' introducing a subordinate clause describing the army. کین = 'vengeance' is the object, and جوید (present/epic present) is 'seeks/sought' — classical narratives often use the present to describe habitual or immediate action. از رزم اسفندیار means 'because of/from the combat with Esfandiyar' (از here gives the cause/relation).
segments.s431-g7: بدو گفت is the common classical formula 'he said to him' where بدو ('to him') precedes گفت ('said'); note that the provided sense-list for گفت did not include 'said', so its lexical sense is treated as the speech verb here. برخیز is an imperative 'rise/stand up'. ازین is the older orthography of 'from this' (این → ین suffix), and خواب خوش = 'pleasant sleep' (adjective follows noun).
segments.s431-g8: برآویز is an imperative meaning 'seize/attack/engage' (literally 'fall upon' in older usage); با marks the companion/target 'with Rostam', and کینه‌کش is an appositive epithet 'the avenger' (lit. 'hatred-killer' — someone who carries out vengeance). The sequence is imperative + prepositional phrase identifying whom to confront.
segments.s431-g73: مکن is the classical prohibitive verb 'do not (make/do)'; it stands before the object, giving a negative command. نام من is a noun + possessor sequence ('name / reputation of me' = 'my name/reputation'); در جهان is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in the world'; زشت و خوار are two coordinating adjectives following the noun ('ugly/vile and despised'), describing the undesired state of the name.
segments.s431-g74: که introduces the subordinate clause giving the reason ('for / because / that'); جز ... نیاید is a fixed negative construction meaning 'nothing except ... comes' (جز = 'except', نیاید = negative present/subjunctive of آمدن, here with a general/inevitable sense 'will come/appear'); ازین is از + این (classical orthography/compound) meaning 'from this'; کارزار is the battlefield / this fight, the source of the bad outcome.
segments.s431-g75: هزارانت is هزاران with an enclitic -ت ('to you'), i.e. 'thousands (to you)'; گوهر is 'gem/jewel' as the direct object; دهم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive form of دادن used here as a promise/future 'I will give'; شاهوار is an epithet/adjective 'regal', here addressed to the king (vocative/epithet).
segments.s431-g76: همان is a demonstrative 'that very / the same'; یاره here means 'beloved / attendant' (an epithet for the ornament or person offered); زر is 'gold' (material); با is the preposition 'with'; گوشوار is 'earring' or ornament—together describing the specific item being offered.
segments.s431-g77: هزارانت again = 'thousands (to you)' (هزاران + enclitic -ت); بنده is 'servant/slave' as the object given; دهم is 'I give' (1st‑person form of دادن, used as promise/future); نوش‌لب is an adjective 'sweet‑lipped' describing those servants.
segments.s431-g78: پرستنده is 'worshipper / devotee' (the role these servants will have); باشد is the 3rd‑person subjunctive/optative 'may they be' or 'let them be' (expressing a wish); ترا is the old clitic form composed of ت (2nd‑person clitic 'to you') + را (here written separately as the enclitic) meaning 'to you'; روز و شب is the time expression 'day and night'.
segments.s431-g79: هزارت is هزار with the enclitic -ت ('a thousand (to you)'); کنیزک is a diminutive 'young maidservant' (the object); دهم is 'I give' (1st‑person form of دادن); خلخی is an ornament (anklet) associated with those maidservants—placed after the noun as a descriptive element.
segments.s431-g80: که here is the relative 'who/which/for' introducing a qualifying clause; زیبای is the adjective 'beautiful'; تاج‌اند is composed of تاج 'crown' + the 3rd‑person plural copula‑ending -اند meaning 'they are crowns' or idiomatically 'they are like crowns' (i.e. 'as beautiful as crowns'); با فرخی uses با 'with' + فرخی 'splendour/fortunate radiance' (adjective/noun) to qualify the beauty. The clause thus praises the maidservants' beauty.
segments.s431-g89: ز + noun + دور کن is an imperative phrase: ز here means 'from' (poetic), دل is 'heart', دور 'away/far', and کن is the 2nd-person imperative 'do (make)'. شهریارا is a vocative ('O king'). تو کین is appended as a vocative + direct object (تو = 'you') plus the noun کین 'hatred/vengeance' — overall: 'O king, put hatred away from the heart (i.e. remove hatred from your heart)'. Note the poetic order places the prepositional phrase (ز دل) before the verb کن.
segments.s431-g90: مکن is the prohibitive (negative imperative) 'do not'. دیو را uses the object marker را to mark دیو ('demon') as the definite object. با + خرد = 'with wisdom', and همنشین is a noun 'companion/associate'. The whole clause reads literally 'Do not (مکن) make the demon (دیو را) companion with wisdom' (i.e. 'do not make the demon a companion of wisdom').
segments.s431-g91: جز از بند دیگر ترا دست هست is an idiomatic, slightly archaic word order: جز از بند دیگر = 'apart from/on other grounds' (بند here = 'segment/respect/point'), ترا is the older object/pronoun form (ت + را) meaning 'you' and دست هست literally 'there is a hand' i.e. 'you have power'. So the clause means 'except in other respects, you have power'. Note the older split ترا (ت + را) and the verbless possession construction 'دست هست' expressing possession.
segments.s431-g92: بمن بر = 'over me' or 'against me' (بمن = 'to/for me', بر = 'on/over'), که introduces the reason/explanation ('for/because'), and the remainder is a predicative list: شاهی و یزدان‌پرست = 'king (kingship/royal) and God-worshipper (pious)'. Together: '...over me, because you are king and pious.'
segments.s431-g93: که introduces the subordinate clause ('for/that'). از بند تا جاودان uses بند in the poetic sense 'stanza/verse' (i.e. 'from this verse') and تا جاودان = 'until eternal' (i.e. 'forever'). نام بد = 'bad name/reputation' (named phrase: noun + adjective). The phrase is a temporal/span expression placing the bad reputation from this stanza onward.
segments.s431-g94: بماند is a subjunctive/3rd‑person verbal form meaning 'will remain' or 'remains' in this context. به من وز تو is a dative pair 'for me and for you' (وز here is an archaic/conjoined form meaning roughly 'and for' — it does not function as the listed literal senses, so no listed sense fits). انجام بد = 'bad outcome/result'. So the clause says 'a bad name will remain, a bad end for me and for you.'
segments.s431-g95: به رستم چنین گفت اسفندیار has an inverted, poetic word order: به رستم = 'to Rostam', چنین = 'thus/so', گفت is the verb 'said' (here used before the subject), and اسفندیار is the subject 'Esfandiyar' placed at the end for emphasis. Literally: 'To Rostam thus said Esfandiyar.' Note: the lemma senses supplied for گفت did not include the verbal 'said' sense, so that entry is treated as undefined in the senses mapping.
segments.s431-g96: که introduces the reported question/continuation ('that'/'how long'). تا + چندگویی here functions as 'until when/how long' (چندگویی = 'talkativeness'/'how long you will speak'), سخن نابکار = 'wicked/ill speech'. Together the clause asks rhetorically 'how long will you keep speaking evil words?' The order is typical of rhetorical questions in the epic style.
segments.s431-g41: بترس is an imperative verb meaning “fear” (addressing the addressee). از introduces the feared object (literally “fear from/of X”). جهاندار یزدان پاک is a sequence of nouns in apposition: جهاندار (literally “world-holder/sovereign”) qualifies the divine name یزدان, and پاک is an adjectival epithet “pure” following the noun it modifies — a common poetic order.
segments.s431-g42: خرد را: خرد (’wisdom’) is made a definite/direct object by the postposed object marker را. مکن is the negative imperative “do not (do).” با دل اندر مغاک is a prepositional phrase; با دل (“with/by heart”) is instrumental, اندر is an archaic preposition meaning “in/into,” and مغاک (“abyss”) is the goal — together: ‘do not (by your heart) put wisdom into the abyss.’
segments.s431-g43: من امروز sets the subject and time (“I, today”). نز بهر is an archaic collocation: نز here functions with بهر to indicate purpose/relation toward the battle (older usage; see note). بهر جنگ آمدم is ‘I came for/to the battle’ — آمدم is the past/perfective first‑person form “I came.”
segments.s431-g44: پی + noun is a prefatory phrase meaning “in pursuit of / seeking.” پی پوزش و نام و ننگ آمدم repeats آمدم for emphasis: ‘I came seeking pوزش (pardon/excuse) and name and nنگ (fame/shame — here the sense leans toward social standing/renown).’ The coordination و links the nouns; the final آمدم resumes the verb from the previous clause for emphasis.
segments.s431-g45: تو is the 2nd‑person subject “you.” با من here functions idiomatically as ‘against me’ or ‘in relation to me.’ به بیداد is an adverbial phrase ‘in injustice/with injustice,’ and کوشی is the 2nd‑person present form of کوشیدن “to try/strive.” همی is an archaic continuous/iterative particle marking ongoing action (‘you keep striving’).
segments.s431-g46: دو چشم خرد را: دو numerally modifies چشم (‘two eyes’), خرد is a genitival noun ‘of wisdom’ and را marks the whole phrase as the definite object. بپوشی is the 2nd‑person present form with the prefix ب- (here meaning ‘you cover/conceal’); together with همی it yields ‘you cover (continually).’ The verb is transitive and takes ‘دو چشم خرد را’ as its object.
segments.s431-g47: A string of oaths/invocations introduced by به: به خورشید و ماه (‘by the sun and the moon’) and then به استا و زند repeats the preposition before two more poetic/archaic terms (here invoked like witnesses or principles). The conjunctions و simply coordinate the items; this is a rhetorical formula calling the named powers to witness.
segments.s431-g48: که introduces the content clause ‘that/so that…’. دل را is the definite object ‘the heart’ (را). نرانی is a negative jussive/imperative form (ن- + verb-stem) meaning ‘do not drive/lead’ (2nd person). به راه گزند is the prepositional goal ‘into the way/path of harm’ (راه = path, گزند = harm). Together: ‘that you do not drive the heart into a harmful path.’
segments.s431-g17: شنیدم is a first-person past verb 'I heard'. که introduces a subordinate clause ('that...'). دستان جادوپرست is a nominal phrase = 'the hands of the magician' (classical Persian often omits the ezāfe -ِ here), with دستان plural of دست followed by the possessor جادوپرست.
segments.s431-g18: به هنگام is an adverbial 'at the time of / when'; یازد is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'opened/extended' or 'gaped' (3rd‑person action linked to دستان). به خورشید دست is a directional/prepositional phrase 'hand toward the sun' = 'reached toward the sun', with the preposition به before خورشید.
segments.s431-g19: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. خشم آرد is 'wrath/anger comes/brings' (آرد = 'brings/comes'), and از جادوان marks the source 'from the magicians/enchanters'; بگذرد here means 'passes/comes forth' or 'is manifested'. The whole clause gives a temporal/background condition: 'when anger comes from the enchanters...'.
segments.s431-g20: برابر نکردم is a past negative 'I did not (برابر) confront/meet/oppose' (برابر here = 'against, opposed to'). پس این با خرد attaches: پس = 'then/so', این = 'this (thing/situation)', با خرد = 'with wisdom'. Together: 'I did not oppose this with wisdom.'
segments.s431-g21: پشوتن is the proper name (the speaker). بدو is the indirect object 'to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it'). گفت here functions as the verb 'said'. پر آب چشم is a descriptive phrase 'full of water of eye' = 'eyes brimming with tears' (پر = 'full (of)', آب چشم = 'tears').
segments.s431-g22: که introduces the content/wish reported. بر دشمنت باد uses باد as an optative particle 'may it be' (not 'wind'); بر دشمنت = 'upon your enemy'. تیمار و خشم are the two nouns being wished upon the enemy: 'may care (treatment/affliction) and anger be upon your enemy.'
segments.s431-g23: چه بودت is a rhetorical/questional classical expression 'what is/was to you' (= 'what has happened to you/what's the matter with you'). که introduces the explanatory clause 'that you are ...'. امروز پژمرده‌ای = 'you are withered/wilted today' (پژمرده‌ای = second person present/past participial description).
segments.s431-g24: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/truly'. به شب خواب here means 'by/at night, sleep' (i.e. 'during the night, (you) sleep'), and نشمرده‌ای is a negated perfect/present 'you have not counted' used idiomatically here as 'you have not slept' (classical usage: not having 'counted' the night = not slept).
segments.s431-g81: This noun group lists the thing and its owners in sequence without an ezafe: 'دگر' (= another/other) modifies 'گنج' (treasure), followed by a series of proper names 'سام نریمان و زال' that specify whose treasure it is. In Classical Persian poetry possessions are often given by simple juxtaposition of names after the noun rather than a modern ezafe phrase.
segments.s431-g82: 'گشایم' is a first‑person form (classical subjunctive/optative morphology) used here with a prospective sense: 'I will open' or 'I will disclose'. 'به پیش تو' is a locative phrase 'in your presence / before you' (به + پیش). The vocative 'تو‌ای' addresses the addressee poetically, and 'بی‌همال' is an epithet applied to that addressee; the whole clause means 'I will open ... before you, O [epithet]'.
segments.s431-g83: 'همه پاک' = 'all the pure/spotless' (adjective after quantifier). 'پیش تو' again means 'in your presence / before you'. The verbal complex 'گرد آورم' is the compound verb 'گرد + آوردن' meaning 'to gather', here in the first person ('I gather / I will gather'); poetically the verb comes after its object phrase.
segments.s431-g84: This short clause gives an additional source: 'ز زابلستان' = 'from Zabulistan' (ز = poetic 'from'). 'نیز' = 'also/likewise'. 'مرد آورم' = 'I will bring men' — 'مرد' here has the sense of courageous/valiant men (heroes), and 'آورم' is first‑person 'I bring'.
segments.s431-g85: 'که تا' here introduces a purpose/result clause: 'so that / in order that'. The sequence 'مر ت را' is an older/clitic sequence meaning 'me and you' / 'me, you' (مر = 'me'; ت = the 2nd‑person clitic 'you'; را = object marker tying them to the verb). 'نیز فرمان کنند' means 'also obey/obey as well' or more literally 'make [them] obey'. In context this clause expresses the desired outcome — that the brought men will be obedient to both addressee and speaker.
segments.s431-g86: 'روان را' is the direct object marked with 'را' (here 'روان' = 'soul' or a person called 'Ravan' in its literal sense). 'به فرمان' is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'by command / at the order (of someone)'. 'گروگان کنند' is the verb phrase 'they make/take (it) hostage' (گروگان = hostage, کنند = they do/make). The construction expresses that, by their orders, the soul/person will be taken as a hostage.
segments.s431-g87: 'ازان پس' = 'after that' (از + آن + پس). 'به پیشت' = 'to/into your presence' (preposition + 'پیش' + enclitic 'ت' = 'your'). 'پرستاروار' is an adjectival compound with the suffix -وار meaning '-like': 'caretaker‑like / guardian‑like', modifying how the speaker will approach.
segments.s431-g88: Two verbs form a vivid compound: 'دوان ... آیم' = 'I will run / rush and come' (دوان = run/rush; آیم = I come). 'با تو' here functions as direction/association 'to you' or 'toward you', and 'بر شهریار' is a vocative/address ('O king') or a directional 'to the king' depending on reading; overall it yields 'I will come running to you, O king.'
segments.s431-g25: میان جهان = a prepositional phrase meaning “in/among the world” placed before the noun phrase for poetic emphasis. این دو یل را is a fronted, definite noun phrase (‘these two heroes’) marked with the object/topic marker را to emphasize or frame them. چه بود is a simple past interrogative (‘what was (there)?’)—چه is the question word and بود is past of بودن.
segments.s431-g26: که introduces a subordinate clause (’that/which’ or explanatory ‘for’). چندین modifies رنج as ‘several/many hardships.’ همی is the archaic continuous/emphatic particle meaning ‘still/always’ or marking repeated action; باید + verb expresses necessity (‘must’). فزود is the classical form of ‘to increase,’ here complement of باید, giving ‘many hardships must increase / be added.’
segments.s431-g27: بدانم is used in the sense ‘I suppose / I hold’ (classical usage of دانستن as ‘to deem’). که introduces the content clause. بخت تو is ‘your fate/fortune’ (possessive order: noun then pronoun). شد is the past of شدن ‘became.’ کندرو is a noun applied predicatively here (literal reading: ‘became incense’), forming the predicate of شدن.
segments.s431-g28: که here supplies the explanatory clause (‘for / because’). کین is the subject (‘vengeance/hatred’) and آورد (present/classical form) is the verb ‘brings / causes.’ هر زمان نو به نو is an adverbial phrase ‘time after time / anew’ (هر = every; نو به نو is an idiom ‘anew, repeatedly’). The clause explains the reason.
segments.s431-g29: بپوشید is a classical narrative past ‘he put on / donned.’ The direct object جوشن (‘cuirass, armor’) appears before the subject phrase in inverted poetic order. یل اسفندیار is a noun phrase in apposition (‘the hero Esfandiyar’), identifying the agent who put on the armor.
segments.s431-g30: بیامد is the past-tense narrative ‘he came.’ بر is the preposition indicating direction/target here (‘against, upon’). رستم is the destination/target and نامدار is an adjective after the name meaning ‘renowned / famed.’ The clause describes his approach toward Rostam.
segments.s431-g31: خروشید is a past narrative verb ‘he roared/cried out.’ چون is used here as a temporal/concessive conjunction (‘when’/‘as’). روی رستم means ‘Rostam’s face’ (روی = face), and بدید is the past ‘he saw.’ The clause gives the occasion for the outcry.
segments.s431-g32: که introduces an exclamatory/optative content clause. نام تو is ‘your name/reputation.’ باد is the classical optative particle (‘may’) expressing a wish or curse. از جهان is the prepositional phrase ‘from the world,’ and ناپدید is a predicate adjective meaning ‘vanished / made to disappear.’ Together: an optative curse ‘May your name be vanished from the world.’
segments.s431-g33: فراموش کردی is a past-tense interrogative phrase (کردی = 'did you [do]') meaning 'did you forget'. تو is an emphatic second-person pronoun placed after the verb (poetic word order). سگزی is an insulting noun/adjective 'cur, doglike one' addressing the addressee; مگر here is a linking particle 'except' that prepares an exception which is completed by the next phrase.
segments.s431-g34: This phrase completes the 'except' begun by مگر: کمان 'bow' and (و) a second item governed by the preposition بر 'against'. بر takes مرد 'man' as its object, and پرخاشخر is an adjectival/nominal qualifier 'quarrelsome' modifying مرد. The conjunction و simply joins the two items of the exception ('your bow and [your actions] against a quarrelsome man').
segments.s431-g35: ز + نیرنگ + زالی is a causal/agent phrase: ز (poetic from/because of = از) + نیرنگ 'stratagem' + زالی 'white‑haired' (here referring to Zāl/the old man). بدین سان is an adverbial phrase 'in this way' (بدین = be- + این, سان = 'so/thus') and درست is a predicative adjective 'right/proper' describing how it was done.
segments.s431-g36: وگرنه 'otherwise' introduces the counterfactual clause begun by که 'that/for'. پایت is 'your foot' (possessive suffix built in). همی is an archaic/adverbial particle that here gives a modal/continuous flavor to the verb (often translated 'would/indeed' in such classical contexts). گور جست is a past tense verb phrase: گور 'grave' + جست 'leapt' — together meaning '(your foot) would have leapt to the grave' (i.e., you would be dead).
segments.s431-g37: بکوبمت is an archaic/poetic first‑person verbal form meaning 'I will strike/beat (you)' (کوبیدن root with archaic ending). زین گونه is a circumstantial phrase 'in this manner/from this kind' (زین = 'from this', گونه = 'manner/way'). امروز is the adverb 'today' and یال ('mane') is the direct object placed at the end for emphasis — metaphorically the addressee's pride or crest.
segments.s431-g38: کزین پس is a temporal phrase 'from this on / henceforth' (کزین = 'from this', پس = 'after/then'). نبیند is the negative present/future form 'will not see' (ن‑ + بیند), with the enclitic ت marking the object 'you' (archaic 2nd‑person object suffix) and را the object marker emphasizing the direct object. زنده modifies the object ('alive') and زال is the subject (Zāl): word order here places the verb before its subject, a common poetic pattern, yielding 'Zāl will not see you alive.'
segments.s431-g39: چنین 'thus' modifies the reporting verb. گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said' (poetic narrative past). The phrase رستم به اسفندیار is the reporter and recipient: رستم is the speaker and به + اسفندیار 'to Esfandiyar'.
segments.s431-g40: که‌ای is a classical relative/vocative formula ('who/you who.../O you who'). سیر 'sated' is here negated by the following ناگشته (an archaic negative participle 'not become / not yet'), so سیر ناگشته means 'not yet sated' or 'unsated'. از کارزار is a prepositional complement 'from the battlefield/war' — together: 'O you who are not yet sated from battle.'
segments.s431-g97: مرا گویی از راه یزدان بگرد — مرا (accusative/oblique clitic) marks the addressee/recipient of the speech ('to me'); گویی is the 2nd-person present 'you say' introducing reported speech; از راه یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'from the way/path of God' (از + راه + یزدان). بگرد is a 2nd-person imperative/subjunctive of گشتن 'turn/leave', giving the content of what is said.
segments.s431-g98: ز فرمان شاه جهانبان بگرد — ز (poetic variant of از) introduces the source 'from the command'; فرمان 'command/decree' is the object; شاه جهانبان is a compound/title 'the king (who) guards the world' (شاه + جهانبان); بگرد repeats the imperative 'turn away' that completes the reported order.
segments.s431-g99: که هرکو ز فرمان شاه جهان — که introduces a relative/conditional clause ('for/that'); هرکو is the archaic fused form of هر که 'whoever/anyone who'; ز فرمان شاه جهان is the prepositional phrase 'from the command of the king of the world' (ز = از). Together they set up the condition 'whoever departs from the king's command'.
segments.s431-g100: بگردد سرآید بدو بر زمان — بگردد is the 3rd-person singular subjunctive/intransitive form of گشتن here meaning 'turns away' or 'departs'; سرآید is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of سرآمدن meaning 'comes/arrives' (i.e. the destined event/fate occurs); بدو 'to him' shows the recipient of that 'coming'; بر زمان literally 'upon time' = 'in time' or 'at the appointed time'. The clause says that the consequence will come upon him in due time.
segments.s431-g101: جز از بند‌گر کوشش (و) کارزار — جز از = 'except from/other than'; بندگر is an agent noun 'binder/maker of bonds' used metaphorically for someone who binds, compels, or resorts to force; کوشش و کارزار are coordinated nouns 'effort/striving and battle/warfare'. The phrase names the kind of response (i.e. mere force and struggle) being excepted.
segments.s431-g102: به پیشم دگرگونه پاسخ میار — به پیشم = 'before me / in my presence' (به + پیشم); دگرگونه 'different/other' modifies پاسخ 'answer/reply'; میار here functions as an imperative/jussive 'bring!' (poetic/persuasive present form used as a command), so the whole segment is a command: 'bring a different answer before me.'
segments.s431-g103: به تندی به پاسخ‌گو نامدار — به تندی is an adverbial phrase 'with haste / vehemently' (preposition به + noun تندی); به پاسخ‌گو نامدار is a prepositional phrase 'to the famed/renowned respondent' (پاسخ‌گو 'respondent/answerer' modified by نامدار 'renowned'). Word order places the adverbial and the indirect object before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s431-g104: چنین گفت کای پرهنر شهریار — چنین گفت 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus' + گفت past tense 'he said'); کای is a vocative particle 'O' (classical/epic usage); پرهنر شهریار is the form of direct address 'skilled/expert sovereign' (پرهنر 'full of skill' modifying شهریار 'king'). The sequence is a standard reporting phrase followed by the quoted address.
segments.s431-g57: برابر here is an adverbial sense 'side by side/equal'; همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle marking ongoing or imminent action; the predication centers on آیم (1st-person present/subjunctive 'I come/go'), with the accompaniment phrase با تو ('with you') and the goal/location phrase به راه ('onto/along the road'). Word order is typical classical Persian (particle + verb + adjuncts).
segments.s431-g58: کنم is first‑person present/subjunctive 'I will do / I shall do' used here as the main verb; هرچ ('هر چه') is a relative/indefinite 'whatever'; فرمان is the noun 'command'; دهی is second‑person present/subjunctive/indicative 'you give', which supplies the content of 'whatever' (i.e. 'whatever command you give'); پیش شاه is a locative/occurrent phrase 'in the presence of / before the king' modifying the act of giving or the situation.
segments.s431-g59: اگر begins a conditional 'if'; کشتنیم is an archaic/classical first‑person plural form of 'کشتن' ('we kill' / 'were we to kill') expressing the hypothetical action; او is the third‑person pronoun 'he'; کشد is a third‑person present subjunctive/indicative form related to the killing/root (here rendered as a potential/result 'may be killed' or similar); شایدم (شاید + م) adds 'perhaps/it may be' and makes the whole clause speculative.
segments.s431-g60: همان 'the same' picks up the foregoing possibility; نیز 'also/likewise' links the two possibilities; اگر is again the conditional 'if'; بند is the noun 'fetter/bond' (i.e. to bind or put in chains); فرمایدم is a polite/classical first‑person past or perfective form of 'فرمودن' meaning 'I ordered/commanded' — here presenting a hypothetical 'if I were to order him bound'.
segments.s431-g61: همی again marks continuous or deliberate action; چاره 'remedy/means' is the direct object of جویم ('I seek', 1st‑person present); که introduces the purpose/result clause; تا here has a purposive sense 'so that/in order that' and links to روزگار, which functions as the subject/agent in the following clause ('fate' or 'time').
segments.s431-g62: ترا is the object marker plus second‑person suffix (ت + را) meaning 'you' as object; سیر here is used in the sense 'to satiate / to make satisfied' (verbal/adjectival meaning); گرداند is the causative/third‑person present 'causes/turns' so the verb phrase 'سیر گرداند' = 'to make (someone) satisfied' or 'to turn (someone) away by making them content'; از کارزار 'from the battle' indicates being removed or turned away from warfare.
segments.s431-g63: نگه کن is an imperative 'look' or 'observe' (نگه + کن); که introduces the subordinate content clause; دانای is an adjectival/noun form 'the wise one'; پیشی here is best read as 'the one before / predecessor' (classical usage) rather than the literal 'cat'; چه is the interrogative 'what'; گفت is the past tense 'he said' (3rd‑person past) introducing the quoted content.
segments.s431-g64: که begins the reported clause; هرگز 'never' with مباد (optative negative particle) creates the wish 'may it never be'; مباد is a classical jussive/optative 'may it not be'; اختر 'star' is the thing mentioned; شوم is first‑person present/subjunctive 'I become' (here in the sense of a wish: 'may I not become'); جفت 'a pair/mate' completes the idea 'become paired/mated to a star' — i.e. 'may I never be joined with a star' (an idiomatic way to express an unwanted matching/omen).
segments.s431-g49: نگیری is a negative present/subjunctive/imperative form (نـ + گیر + ـی) meaning 'do not take / do not hold'; به یاد is a fixed prepositional phrase 'in/to the memory of' (به + یاد). آن سخنها که رفت is a relative clause 'those words that have gone/are gone' with که as the relative 'that' and رفت used in the sense 'have vanished/are past.'
segments.s431-g50: وگر introduces a conditional 'but if / even if'. پوٌست بر تن کسی را is the object phrase (پوست = 'skin', بر تن = 'from/on the body', کسی را = 'someone' with the object marker را). بکفت is a poetic/archaic perfect-conditional sense 'would have been enough' — the verb expresses the hypothetical result in classical Persian.
segments.s431-g51: بیابی and ببینی are optative/subjunctive or hortative forms ('may you find', 'may you see') rather than plain infinitives; the doubling (بیابی ببینی) is emphatic/poetic. یکی خان من = 'a certain/a single Khan of mine' with یکی as 'a certain' and خان as the title 'khan/lord'; من is the possessive pronoun 'my/I'.
segments.s431-g52: روندست is a descriptive predicate attached to the preceding noun phrase ('is in motion / is active' in this reading — an adjectival/predicative use). کام تو is a genitival/possessive phrase 'your desire' (کام = desire, تو = you). بر جان من is a prepositional phrase 'upon my life/soul' indicating that that desire rests or is laid on the speaker — word order is typical Persian: possessor then target.
segments.s431-g53: گشایم is 1st person singular verb form ('I open / I will open') in a classical/poetic conjugation. در here functions as the noun 'door' (not the preposition) and combines with گنج دیرینه 'ancient treasure/treasury' to make 'the door of the ancient treasury'; باز at the end is the adverb 'again' modifying the opening action.
segments.s431-g54: کجا گرد کردم is a rhetorical past question 'where have I gone/about/wandered' — گرد with کردن here means 'to go about, to wander' and کردم is the 1st person past. به سال دراز is a duration adverbial 'through/for long years' (به + سال + دراز), placed after the verb as is common in Persian.
segments.s431-g55: کنم is 1st person present/subjunctive 'I do / I will do' used here to express intent. بار بر بارگیهای خویش is an object phrase 'a load upon my own foliage/branches' (بار = load; بر = 'on/upon' as preposition; بارگیهای = plural 'leaves/branches'; خوی + ش is the possessive root + enclitic 'his/her/its' used here as 'my/one's own' in older usage). The phrase literally pictures piling loads on the speaker's own boughs.
segments.s431-g56: به indicates the destination 'to' (to the treasury). گنجور is the treasury/treasure-house (dative). تا here introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that' (classical use). براند is a subjunctive/optative or present form 'may drive away / drive out' (rān-dan root), and ز پیش is the prepositional phrase 'from before / out of the front'. Together this gives a purpose: 'give to the treasury so that it drives [them] away from before.'

sentences.s431-s53: You continually hold my words in contempt; with a fixed stare you seek to harm me.
sentences.s431-s54: Thus he answered: 'How long will you persist in deceit? Verily the descent has entered a narrow place.'
sentences.s431-s5: When Esfandiyar heard his voice, the armament of the world became despised before him.
sentences.s431-s6: Then he said thus to Peshotan: that the lion may turn away from the claw of the brave man.
sentences.s431-s7: I did not suppose that Rostam would, from the road, drag into the portico a tiger and an infidel and a hat.
sentences.s431-s8: That same load-bearing Rakhsh — beneath him there was not a single arrow visible upon him.
sentences.s431-s33: He answered thus: a deceitful man—half the day given to reproach and the day to rebuke.
sentences.s431-s34: If you wish to live and remain in your place, restrain your first word; do not set foot forward.
sentences.s431-s35: From the iwan and the khan, how often do you speak—always washing the face of reconciliation.
sentences.s431-s36: Once more Rostam opened his tongue: “O king, do not recall injustice.”
sentences.s431-s1: At that very moment when the dawn rose, it shone into the midst of the dark night.
sentences.s431-s2: Rostam put on his battle-gear, and kept remembering the Creator of the world.
sentences.s431-s3: When he came upon the renowned army that sought vengeance because of the fight with Esfandiyar.
sentences.s431-s4: He said to him, 'Rise from this pleasant sleep; engage with Rostam, the avenger.'
sentences.s431-s37: Do not make my name in the world vile and despised, for nothing but ill will come from this battle.
sentences.s431-s38: I will give you thousands of gems, O lofty king — that very golden ornament with earrings.
sentences.s431-s39: I will give you thousands of servants, sweet‑lipped; may they be your worshippers day and night.
sentences.s431-s40: I will give you a thousand maidservants with anklets, who are as beautiful as crowns in their splendour.
sentences.s431-s45: O king, put hatred far from your heart; do not make the demon a companion of wisdom.
sentences.s431-s46: In other matters you have power over me, for you are king and God‑worshipper.
sentences.s431-s47: For from this stanza until eternity a bad name will remain — a bad outcome for me and for you.
sentences.s431-s48: Esfandiyar said to Rostam thus: 'How long will you keep uttering wicked words?'
sentences.s431-s21: Fear the world-ruler, the pure Yezdan; do not cast wisdom into the abyss with your heart.
sentences.s431-s22: Today I have come to the battle; I have come seeking neither excuse nor mere safety, but for name and renown.
sentences.s431-s23: You strive against me with injustice; you would cover (blind) the two eyes of wisdom.
sentences.s431-s24: By the sun and the moon, and by Esta and Zend, do not drive the heart into the path of harm.
sentences.s431-s9: I heard that the magician's hands, at the moment they opened, reached out toward the sun.
sentences.s431-s10: When wrath comes from the enchanters, I did not confront it with wisdom.
sentences.s431-s11: Peshotan, his eyes full of water, said to him, 'May care and wrath be upon your enemy.'
sentences.s431-s12: What has happened to you that you are wilted today? Truly, you did not sleep at night.
sentences.s431-s41: I will open another treasure — the treasure of Sam, Nariman and Zal — before you, O peerless one.
sentences.s431-s42: I will gather all the pure ones before you; from Zabulistan I will also bring men (valiant men).
sentences.s431-s43: so that they may also obey you and me; they will, by command, take the soul as hostage.
sentences.s431-s44: After that, I will run into your presence like a guardian and come to you, O king.
sentences.s431-s13: What was it between these two champions in the world that would make many more hardships increase?
sentences.s431-s14: I take it that your fortune has turned to incense,
sentences.s431-s15: The hero Esfandiyar donned his cuirass and came against the famed Rostam.
sentences.s431-s16: He roared when he saw Rostam’s face: ‘May your name be wiped from the world.’
sentences.s431-s17: Have you forgotten, you cur — except for your bow and your assaults on quarrelsome men?
sentences.s431-s18: By Zāl’s stratagem you contrived it thus; otherwise your foot would already have leapt into the grave.
sentences.s431-s19: I will strike (you) today in this manner — your mane — so that henceforth Zāl will not see you alive.
sentences.s431-s20: Thus Rostam said to Esfandiyar: 'O you who are not yet sated from the battlefield,'
sentences.s431-s49: You tell me, “Turn away from the path of God; turn away from the command of the king, guardian of the world.”
sentences.s431-s50: For whoever turns away from the king’s command will, in time, have that fate come upon him.
sentences.s431-s51: Unless (what you mean is) binding/striving and battle, bring me before you a different answer.
sentences.s431-s52: Swiftly, to the famed respondent he said thus: 'O skilled sovereign.'
sentences.s431-s29: I will go with you side by side on the road, and I will do whatever command you give before the king.
sentences.s431-s30: If we kill him, he may be killed, perhaps; likewise, if I ordered him bound.
sentences.s431-s31: I will seek a remedy so that fate may turn you away from the battlefield.
sentences.s431-s32: Look—see what the wise predecessor said: may I never be joined as a mate to a star.
sentences.s431-s25: Do not dwell on those words that have passed; and even if they tore the skin from someone's body, that would have sufficed.
sentences.s431-s26: You may find, you may see a certain Khan of mine; he is on the move — your wish rests upon my life.
sentences.s431-s27: I will open again the door of the ancient treasury; where have I wandered in the long years?
sentences.s431-s28: I will heap load upon my own boughs; give (them) to the treasury so that it may drive them away from before.
